paper_title: The Shadow Price of Reasoning: Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Inference-time scaling has emerged as a critical avenue for enhancing Large Language Models' performance, yet real-world deployment is constrained by strict computational budgets. In this work, we formulate inference budget allocation as a global constrained optimization problem governed by economic principles. By modeling per-query reasoning utility with a shifted-surge function, we derive an optimal allocation policy based on a global shadow price that equilibrates marginal utility under resource scarcity. Based on this theory, we propose Constrained Latent-utility Equilibrium Allocation for Reasoning (CLEAR). It performs rational abandonment and reallocates resources from insolvent queries to solvable queries near their emergence thresholds. Extensive experiments on several reasoning tasks with different traffic streams demonstrate that CLEAR significantly improves the Pareto frontier of total token cost versus mean accuracy. In resource-scarce regimes, CLEAR achieves up to a 3x improvement in global accuracy compared to uniform allocation.

sections.content: The Shadow Price of Reasoning: Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Xu Wan * 1 2 3 Speed Zhu * 2 Jianwei Cai 2 Guang Chen 2 Ximing Huang 2 Wiggin Zhou 2 Mingyang Sun 3 6 2 0 2 2 ] . [ 1 2 9 0 3 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Inference-time scaling has emerged as critical avenue for enhancing Large Language Models performance, yet real-world deployment is constrained by strict computational budgets. In this work, we formulate inference budget allocation as global constrained optimization problem governed by economic principles. By modeling per-query reasoning utility with shifted-surge function, we derive an optimal allocation policy based on global shadow price that equilibrates marginal utility under resource scarcity. Based on this theory, we propose Constrained Latent-utility Equilibrium Allocation for Reasoning (CLEAR). It performs rational abandonment and reallocates resources from insolvent queries to solvable queries near their emergence thresholds. Extensive experiments on several reasoning tasks with different traffic streams demonstrate that CLEAR significantly improves the Pareto frontier of total token cost versus mean accuracy. In resource-scarce regimes, CLEAR achieves up to 3 improvement in global accuracy compared to uniform allocation. The code is available in Here. 1. Introduction The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) is undergoing paradigm shift from training-time scaling to inference-time scaling (Wei et al., 2022; Snell et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2024). Recent breakthroughs demonstrate that increasing test-time compute can yield performance gains comparable to scaling model parameters by invoking System 2 reasoning capabilities (Weston & Sukhbaatar, 2023; Brown et al., 2024). series of length-scaling experiments 1Zhejiang University 2Tecent HY Team 3Peking University. Correspondence to: Mingyang Sun <smy@pku.edu.cn>. Proceedings of the 43 rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026. Copyright 2026 by the author(s). 1 Figure 1. The S-Shaped Compute-Utility Curve. We evaluate Qwen2.5-Math-7B (Yang et al., 2024) on three benchmarks. The budget-performance relationship exhibits three distinct regions: (1) pre-threshold Strict phase with negligible utility; (2) rapid Surge phase offering high leverage; and (3) an Ample phase characterized by diminishing returns. has furthermore validated that by allowing models to think longer, it leads to substantial improvements in complex reasoning tasks (Muennighoff et al., 2025; Aggarwal & Welleck, 2025). However, in real-world deployment scenarios, test-time compute is finite and expensive commodity. Whether serving millions of concurrent users via cloud APIs or running models on resource-constrained edge devices, practitioners operate under strict global budget constraints (Chen et al., 2023). The central challenge thus shifts from merely increasing the theoretical ceiling of model intelligence to maximizing global utility under fixed computational cap (Raposo et al., 2024). Standard deployment practices usually impose uniform policy, assigning the same generation limit (e.g., fixed max new tokens) to every query. This implicitly assumes that all queries share comparable compute-utility profile, which conflicts with the large heterogeneity of reasoning tasks. As illustrated in Figure 1, reasoning utility follows an S-shaped curve when we fix the decoding strategy and vary the token limit. For difficult queries like AIME, uniform limit may leave the trajectory in the Strict phase, where compute is spent but utility remains near zero. For easy queries like GSM8K, the same limit may push generation into the Ample phase, where additional tokens bring only diminishing returns. Of course, in an unconstrainedEconomic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Figure 2. Empirical Rollouts and Latent Utility. For selected AIME-24 problems, blue bars show the number of correct rollouts in each length bin, while red curves depict the fitted latent utility mapping induced by our shifted-surge model. resource setting, the inefficiency caused by pushing easy queries into the Ample phase may be less consequential. However, this work focuses on the resource-constrained setting, where fixed total token budget is imposed over the entire evaluation set. The central question is therefore how to allocate tokens across individual queries so as to maximize overall token utility under this global budget. We therefore treat inference budgeting as batch-level allocation problem: given fixed total token supply, decide how many tokens each query should receive to maximize aggregate expected utility. This gives rise to global constrained optimization problem over instance-specific utility curves. Although the resulting objective is non-convex, its Lagrangian form reveals simple economic principle: at optimum, every active query should spend tokens until its marginal utility matches common global shadow price (Boyd & Vandenberghe, 2004; Devanur et al., 2019). Queries whose attainable marginal gain never clears this price should receive no budget, while the remaining queries share the budget according to their utility slopes. Building on these findings, we propose Constrained Latentutility Equilibrium Allocation for Reasoning (CLEAR). Instead of uniform utility for each query, CLEAR maps it into surge-shaped utility curve. The system then operates as computational market: (1) Threshold Modeling: We estimate the emergence threshold of each query and instantiate its latent utility curve; (2) Price Discovery: We employ fast bisection search to find the unique global shadow price that clears the market, ensuring the total demand matches the available budget; (3) Optimal Allocation: Based on the discovered price, we apply closed-form policy derived from the Lambert function to strictly determine the token limit for each query, automatically handling truncation or rational abandonment. Crucially, CLEAR requires no retraining of the backbone LLM and operates as plug-and-play inference wrapper. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We identify three-phase compute-utility pattern in LLM reasoning and formalize inference-time token allocation over non-concave latent utility curves, where the optimal policy is governed by global shadow price. (2) We derive closed-form Lambert allocation policy and instantiate it in CLEAR, plug-and-play framework that combines latent-threshold prediction with market-clearing price discovery. (3) We validate CLEAR on mixed-complexity mathematical reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating improved costaccuracy Pareto efficiency and robustness to hyperparameter choices and predictor noise. 2. Empirical Motivation To better model the per-query utility of reasoning tokens, we investigate whether longer reasoning is uniformly beneficial for each query. If every problem has an instance-specific favorable reasoning length, then inference utility should naturally rise after minimum threshold but eventually saturate or decline when generation becomes excessive. Following the thoughtology analysis (Marjanovic et al., 2025) in DeepSeek-R1, we conduct controlled experiment using Qwen2.5-Math-7B. We perform sampling with high temperature = 1.0 to induce diverse reasoning paths of varying lengths. We generate = 50 responses for AIME-24 and = 4 for the GSM8K, MATH-500 benchmarks, and group the resulting trajectories into length bins to compute the conditional Pass@1 accuracy. As visualized in Figure 2, reasoning utility exhibits nonlinear dynamics rather than simple scaling. We identify three recurring regimes: Strict phase where short trajectories fail consistently, implying minimum solvable threshold; Surge phase where performance rises sharply 2 Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs past this threshold; and an Ample phase where additional generation yields diminishing returns and may eventually degrade solution quality. 3. Problem Formulation Based on the empirical observations above, we first model the per-query reasoning utility with function that captures the StrictSurgeAmple structure. Under this utility model, the problem of assigning tokens subject to fixed total budget naturally becomes global constrained optimization problem with an instance-dependent, non-concave utility landscape. 3.1. Modeling the Physics of Reasoning While the observed outcome of reasoning task is binary (correct or incorrect), we posit that this outcome is governed by continuous, unobservable variable: the reasoning utility ϕ(t). For query si, we model the accumulated potential after generating tokens follows Shifted Surge Function: ϕi(t) = (cid:40) 0 αi(t τi) eβi(tτi) 0 < τi τi (1) This parameterization aligns the latent utility model with the three empirical regimes observed above: Strict: Utility remains zero until the generation length crosses the latent emergence threshold τi. Surge: Once the threshold is crossed, reasoning potential rises with initial velocity αi, capturing the rapid accumulation of valid reasoning. Ample: When generation extends excessively, the exponential decay term eβit dominates, reflecting diminishing returns. In Figure 2, the red curve visualizes the fitted latent reasoning potential, which provides continuous utility mapping from the empirical rollout outcomes observed at different generation lengths. 3.2. The Global Optimization Objective We adopt pure token-based cost model where the cost Ci(t) = t. The systems goal is to allocate vector of tokens = [t1, . . . , tN ] for queries to maximize aggregate reasoning potential under global token budget limit Btotal: max tRN 0 s.t. (cid:88) i=1 (cid:88) i= ϕi(ti) ti Btotal. (2) 3 4. Theoretical Analysis To solve the non-convex optimization problem in Eq. (2), we apply the method of Lagrange multipliers. By relaxing the global budget constraint, we establish an economic principle governing optimal inference: the equalization of marginal reasoning potential. 4.1. Shadow Price Parity We construct the Lagrangian L(t, λ) = (cid:80) ϕi(ti) λ((cid:80) ti Btotal), where λ 0 is the Lagrange multiplier. In economic terms, λ represents the global shadow price of computationthe marginal gain in total potential obtained by relaxing the budget by one token. The Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions imply that for any globally optimal allocation t, the marginal gain of each active task must align with this global price. This parity determines whether query should be lifted out of Strict, placed near its high-leverage Surge, or capped before wasting budget in Ample. We formalize this as: Definition 4.1 (Shadow Price Parity). strictly positive budget allocation > 0 is local maximum if it satisfies the first-order stationarity condition, equalizing the marginal reasoning potential to the global shadow price: ϕi(t ) ti = λ. (3) The global optimal policy is then determined by comparing the utility surplus at this stationary point against the abandonment option (ti = 0). We term this the Rational Abandonment Condition, where tasks whose maximum net surplus falls below zero are deemed economically insolvent and assigned zero budget. 4.2. Individual Optimal Allocation Policy For the surge potential ϕi(t) = αiteβit, where = τi, the shadow price parity condition yields the differential equation: dϕi dt = αieβit(1 βit) = λ. (4) While standard algebraic methods cannot solve for in this transcendental equation, we prove that the exact solution is given by the Lambert function (z), defined as the inverse of (w) = wew. Theorem 4.2 (Individual Optimal Allocation Policy). Under the shadow price parity condition, the optimal token allocation for given λ follows closed-form solution: (λ) = τi + 1 βi (cid:20) 1 W0 (cid:19)(cid:21) , (cid:18) λe αi (5) Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs subject to the solvency constraint ϕi(t . Here, W0() denotes the principal branch of the Lambert function, and is Eulers number. ) > λt Proof. The objective for each task is to maximize the net economic surplus Ji(t), defined as the difference between the latent reasoning potential and the opportunity cost of tokens: Ji(t) = ϕi(t) λt = αi(t τi)eβi(tτi) λt. We assume the task is in the active reasoning phase (t τi). Let = τi denote the effective generation length. The first-order necessary condition for optimality requires the marginal potential to equal the shadow price: (6) dϕi dt = λ. (7) Applying the product rule to differentiate the surge function with respect to t: dt (cid:2)αiteβit(cid:3) = αi (cid:0)1 eβit + (βi)eβit(cid:1) = αieβit(1 βit). (8) Equating this to λ, we obtain the transcendental equation: αieβit(1 βit) = λ. To solve for t, we transform this equation into the canonical form of the Lambert function, wew = z. We introduce change of variable: (9) = 1 βit. (10) This implies βit = 1 u, and consequently, the exponential term becomes eβit = e(1u) = eu1. Substituting these into the optimality condition: ueue1 = αi eu1 = λ λ αi λe αi ueu = (11) . This interior solution is valid if and only if the resulting net surplus is positive (Ji(t ) > 0). If the surplus is nonpositive, the global optimum defaults to the boundary solution = 0. 4.3. Global Equilibrium and Phase Transitions The Lambert policy derived in Eq. (5) describes the local optimum for fixed price λ. However, the inference system operates under hard global constraint. The global optimal policy is realized by finding the unique market-clearing price λ that satisfies the resource budget: (cid:88) i=1 (λ) = Btotal. (14) Since (λ) is strictly monotonically decreasing with respect to λ, unique λ is guaranteed to exist. The magnitude of the global budget Btotal inversely determines λ. This induces three system-level allocation regimes, which correspond to different operating points on the per-query StrictSurgeAmple utility curves: 1. Abundant-Budget Regime. When Btotal is large, the shadow price λ approaches zero. In this limit, the Lambert term vanishes: W0(0) = 0, and the allocation converges to ti τi + 1/βi. Active tasks are therefore allowed to approach the peak of their utility curves, near the transition from Surge to Ample. 2. Scarce-Budget Regime. As Btotal tightens, λ rises. The term W0( λe ) increases, compressing the allocation αi toward the threshold τi. The system thus prioritizes queries whose allocated tokens can still land in the high-marginalreturn Surge segment. 3. Abandonment Regime. When Btotal is critically low, λ exceeds the initial velocity αi for difficult tasks (λ > αi). No positive allocation can generate enough marginal surplus to justify crossing the threshold, so such tasks are abandoned and remain before their Strict threshold with indicator function I() = 0. By the definition of the Lambert function, the solution for is given by the principal branch W0, as we require the solution corresponding to the physical expansion phase: 5. Methodology = (cid:19) . (cid:18) λe αi (12) Finally, we substitute back to solve for the optimal allocation : 1 βi(t τi) = W0 (cid:19) (cid:18) λe αi βi(t τi) = 1 W0 = τi + 1 βi (cid:19) (cid:18) λe αi (cid:20) 1 W0 (cid:19)(cid:21) . (cid:18) λe αi In this section, we translate the theoretical optimal policy derived in Theorem 4.2 into CLEAR, practical inferencetime control algorithm. Unlike heuristic methods that rely on manual rules for truncation or abandonment, CLEAR numerically solves for the shadow price λ that equilibrates the aggregate demand of the batch with the global supply of tokens. (13) 5.1. Threshold Prediction and Parametric Scaling The core input to our Lambert policy is the emergence threshold τi, which captures the minimum computation reEconomic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs quired for query si to enter the productive reasoning regime. We employ lightweight predictor fθ : R+ based on DeBERTa-v3-base to estimate this value: ˆτi = exp(fθ(si)). (15) To map these predictions into actionable utility curves, CLEAR relies on two critical parameters that govern the systems behavior: Initial Velocity α: This parameter defines the initial magnitude of the utility functions derivative at the predicted threshold ˆτ . It functions as the systems reservation price: any query where the global market-clearing price λ exceeds α yields negative net surplus and is assigned zero tokens. Decay Rate β: This parameter governs the exponential rate at which marginal utility diminishes beyond the predicted length ˆτ . It is inversely proportional to the characteristic length of the token allocation window. To accommodate varying traffic conditions, we implement an adaptive mechanism where β is derived dynamically from the aggregate budget surplus: ceiling; any price λ > α yields negative surplus for all tasks, resulting in zero allocation. Candidate Evaluation: In each iteration, we propose candidate price λmid and compute the optimal allocation for every query using the Lambert Policy (Eq. 5). This involves three substeps: first, computing the unconstrained surge length via the principal branch W0 using the adaptive β; second, enforcing the solvency condition by zeroing out allocations where the cost λ exceeds the utility (Rational Abandonment); and third, applying the physical context limit Tmax. Market Clearing Update: We aggregate the individual allocations to determine the total consumption Ctotal. If Ctotal exceeds the global budget Btotal, indicating excess demand, we raise the price by setting λmin λmid. Conversely, if there is supply surplus, we lower the price by setting λmax λmid. The full procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1. Note that the Lambert function W0(z) is efficiently computed via standard numerical libraries, thus introducing negligible latency compared to the autoregressive generation process. β = 1 max(ϵ, τ ) . (16) 6. Experimental Settings Here, is the designated budget constraint and τ is the mean predicted budget. Remarks. While Theorem 4.2 provides general solution for task-specific shaping parameters {αi, βi}, predicting βi of an unseen query is computationally intractable and prone to high variance. Therefore, we adopt model-intrinsic assumption: we treat α and β as global hyperparameters that characterize the average reasoning dynamics of the LLM backbone itself, rather than the specific query. We assume task heterogeneity is primarily captured by the threshold parameter τi, which we predict dynamically. 5.2. Global Shadow Price Optimization The core of CLEAR lies in identifying the precise λ that saturates the global budget constraint. Leveraging the monotonicity established in Section 4.3, where the individual optimal allocation (λ) strictly decreases with respect to the shadow price, we guarantee that the aggregate consumption function C(λ) = (cid:80) (λ) is invertible. Therefore, we solve λ using the bisection method, proceeding in three distinct steps: Search Space Initialization: We define the search interval [λmin, λmax]. The lower bound λmin = 0 represents the saturation regime (infinite budget). The upper bound λmax = α corresponds to the total abandonment regime. Crucially, α serves as the theoretical and Training Data We Models emQwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct ploy and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct (Yang et al., 2025) as the frozen backbones for all reasoning tasks. To estimate the latent emergence threshold τ (s), we fine-tune DeBERTa-v3-base encoder as the threshold predictor. The predictor is trained to regress the logarithmic token length of the models generated solutions, which serves as proxy for the emergence threshold. The training data is drawn exclusively from splits of GSM8K and MATH. Evaluation Datasets and Streams As illustrated in Figure 3, we evaluate on mixed oracle pool spanning MATH-500, AMC-23, AIME-24, AIME-25, Minerva, and OlympiadBench, and construct four synthetic inference streams: Balanced, Mostly-Easy, Mostly-Hard, and U-Shaped. The detailed training and evaluation setting is shown in Table 6 and 7. Baselines and Allocation Policies We benchmark CLEAR (Lambert) 1 against several allocation strategies. Uniform evenly distributes the global budget across all queries, while Predictor assigns tokens proportional to the predicted threshold ˆτi but does not support abandonment. TALE-EP (Han et al., 2025) serves as an external 1We use CLEAR (Lambert) to denote the full CLEAR pipeline proposed above, distinguishing it from the internal CLEAR variants used for ablation. 5 Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Figure 3. Oracle-Length Distribution across Evaluation Traffic Streams. Each panel shows one synthetic traffic stream (Balanced, Mostly-Easy, Mostly-Hard, and U-Shaped), with n=500 queries sampled from the 7B oracle pool. length-prediction baseline that estimates per-query token demand and renormalizes allocations to the same global budget. We also compare against two internal ablations: CLEAR (Heuristic), which applies an affine allocation with median-based rejection cutoff, and CLEAR (Auction), which greedily admits queries by predicted return on investment under the budget constraint. Finally, Oracle provides an upper bound using ground-truth solution lengths. Detailed formulations are provided in Appendix A.2. 7. Results and Analysis 7.1. Latent Threshold Modeling Accurately predicting the exact token consumption for reasoning tasks is inherently challenging. However, for our resource allocation purpose, the global shadow price λ naturally acts as normalizer, absorbing absolute prediction errors. Thus, the allocation primarily relies on the predictors ability to correctly rank tasks by latent threshold. Figure 4 demonstrates the log-scale regression performance of our predictor. Figure 4. Predictor Performance Analysis. (Left) Log-log scatter plot of predicted vs. actual token lengths. High rank correlation coefficients (Spearmans ρ, Kendalls τ ) indicate the model effectively captures relative threshold. (Right) Stratified comparison of token count distributions across threshold tiers. 7.2. Main Performance Comparison Comparison on Mathematical Reasoning. Figure 6 and Table 1 summarizes the accuracy of CLEAR compared to the Uniform, Predictor-based, and Heuristic and Auctionbased variants. The largest improvements occur under the 6 most constrained budget of 256 tokens per query. In the Balanced stream, CLEAR improves over the Uniform policy by +11.6 accuracy points. In the Mostly-Easy stream, CLEAR achieves an even larger gain of +24.0 accuracy points, indicating that uniform allocation wastes scarce tokens on queries that cannot produce sufficient utility under the global budget. In the Mostly-Hard and U-Shaped streams, CLEAR further improves over Uniform by +5.2 and +14.2 points, respectively. As the budget increases, the absolute gains naturally shrink because most methods receive enough tokens to cross the latent emergence threshold. This trend confirms that CLEAR is most beneficial in resource-scarce regimes, where shadow-price allocation and rational abandonment prevent the system from spreading compute too thinly across the batch. Generalization to Code Generation To examine whether the proposed allocation principle extends beyond mathematical reasoning, we evaluate CLEAR on code-generation tasks. We use Qwen2.5-Coder-7B and retrain the length regressor in-domain using completion lengths from HumanEval and MBPP, while keeping the allocation algorithm unchanged. Results are reported under bestof-K protocol with = 4: each trajectory has cap of = 1024, and each query receives the same total completion-token budget of 4B across methods. As shown in Table 2, CLEAR consistently improves over uniform allocation across all three code benchmarks. This suggests that global budget clearing and strategic abandonment are not limited to the math-focused setting, but can also benefit stochastic code generation under strict compute constraints. Visualization of Token Allocation To understand the mechanism behind the performance of CLEAR, we visualize the token allocation in Figure 6. Under the scarcity regime of 256 tokens, the Uniform and Predictor-based policies distribute the budget continuously but insufficiently across all queries. Consequently, the majority of tasks receive fewer tokens than their groundtruth reasoning length, leading to systemic failure. In conEconomic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Table 1. Comprehensive Performance Comparison across Evaluation Streams. We compare CLEAR (Lambert) against various baselines including Uniform allocation, Proportional Predictor, and two CLEAR variants. The Oracle represents the theoretical upper bound. Bold indicates the best practical performance among non-oracle methods. The bottom row highlights the Absolute Accuracy Gain of CLEAR (Lambert) over the Uniform. The star markers () indicate the operating points automatically selected by our adaptive β mechanism. Figure 8. Scale Invariance of the Initial Velocity α. The left axis (grey) shows that accuracy remains effectively constant. The right axis (blue dashed) reveals the optimized shadow price λ scales linearly with α (log λ log α). Table 2. Code Generation Results. Accuracy comparison under aligned best-of-4 completion-token budgets. over Uniform even at high noise levels. Method HumanEval+ MBPP+ BigCodeBench Uniform CLEAR (Lambert) Gain (Abs.) 36.8 43.1 +6. 39.4 45.9 +6.5 16.2 19.8 +3.6 trast, CLEAR explicitly abandons tasks where the computational cost outweighs the potential gain. Crucially, the resources conserved from these discarded tasks are redistributed to the admitted queries. Unlike binary cut-off or auction, the Lambert policy dynamically adjusts the extra allowance based on the decay rate β, ensuring valid tasks receive ample resources to handle uncertainty. 7.3. Robustness and Sensitivity We further analyze the robustness of CLEAR across three dimensions: phase transitions, predictor noise, and hyperparameter sensitivity. Robustness to Predictor Noise. Figure 11 shows the accuracy degradation as we inject log-normal noise into the predictor. While performance naturally declines with increasing noise, CLEAR maintains significant advantage 8 Sensitivity to Hyperparameters. We examine the systems sensitivity to its two core economic parameters: the decay rate β and the initial velocity α. Figure 7 evaluates the impact of static β values. We observe distinct regime shift: low-budget scenarios require strict, high β to minimize waste and ensure solvency for easier tasks, whereas high-budget scenarios benefit from lenient, low β that allows for extended reasoning surges. Therefore, static β cannot simultaneously satisfy these conflicting demands. Our adaptive mechanism dynamically calibrates β based on the aggregate surplus, consistently landing on the Pareto-optimal frontier across all regimes without manual tuning. Unlike β, the threshold α exhibits unique scale-invariant property. As shown in Figure 8, varying α results in negligible fluctuations in accuracy. Meanwhile, the market-clearing shadow price λ scales linearly with α. Since the Lambert allocation depends on the ratio λ/α, the optimizer automatically adjusts λ to offset any changes in α. 7.4. Structural Utility Variants To test whether the gains of CLEAR depend on the exact shifted-surge parameterization, we evaluate two alternative Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs utility structures that preserve the same threshold-aware allocation principle but replace the latent utility shape. The Triangular variant uses tent-shaped utility that rises linearly after the predicted threshold and then decays linearly after its peak. The Quadratic variant uses concave quadratic peak over the post-threshold extension. In both cases, the system still solves for global budgetclearing allocation and applies rational abandonment under tight budgets. Let = τi denote the effective post-threshold generation length, ci denote the maximum supported post-threshold extension, pi (0, ci) denote the triangular peak location, and umax denote the peak utility. The Triangular utility is defined as: ϕTri (t) = 0 umax umax 0 pi cit cipi 0 < τi 0 pi pi < ci > ci. (17) The Quadratic utility uses smooth concave peak over the same post-threshold support: ϕQuad (t) = 0 umax 4t(cit) c2 0 < τi 0 ci > ci. (18) Table 3. Structural Utility Variant Results. Accuracy comparison under the tight-budget setting ( = 256). Method Balanced Mostly-Hard U-Shaped Uniform CLEAR (Lambert) Triangular (Lambert) Quadratic (Lambert) 5.4 19.4 18.8 19.4 2.2 13.4 13.0 13.4 8.2 23.8 21.6 23.6 Figure 9. Visualization of Structural Utility Variants. Table 3 shows that both structural variants remain substantially above uniform allocation in the most resourceconstrained regime. This suggests that the main benefit comes from global budget clearing and abandonment-aware allocation, rather than from single fragile choice of utility curve. 9 8. Related Work Resource Allocation for LLM Inference. LLM deployment is increasingly studied as resource allocation problem, where computation, latency, and tokens are scarce assets to be assigned under global constraints. Systems such as FrugalGPT route queries through model cascades to reduce cost (Chen et al., 2023), while RouteLLM learns preference-based routing policies across models (Ong et al., 2024). Other work formulates model selection or query allocation as contextual bandits, knapsack optimization, or cascade scheduling, including LLM-Bandit and TREACLE (Zhang et al., 2024). Complementary infrastructure analyses characterize the hardware-level Pareto frontier of throughput, latency, and energy (Kwon et al., 2023; Sheng et al., 2023; Wilhelm et al., 2025). Efficient CoT. CoT prompting and inference-time scaling show that allocating more computation at test time can substantially improve reasoning performance (Wei et al., 2022; Snell et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2024; Wan et al., 2025). related line of work improves token efficiency by adapting the reasoning procedure itself. DSC (Wang et al., 2025) allocates different numbers of sampled reasoning paths according to question difficulty and posterior confidence. SelfBudgeter (Li et al., 2025) trains the model to estimate and obey instance-specific reasoning budgets, reducing unnecessary overthinking. TALE (Han et al., 2025) estimates token budget for each problem and injects it into the prompt to guide shorter CoT generation. These methods focus on controlling or aggregating reasoning traces within query. In contrast, CLEAR addresses batch-level allocation problem: given fixed decoding behavior and fixed global token budget, it decides how much budget each query should receive and when query should be rationally abandoned. 9. Conclusion In this work, we study how to allocate fixed inferencetime token budget across heterogeneous reasoning queries. We show that uniform budgeting can waste tokens on hard queries that cannot be solved within the available budget, while overlooking queries that could be solved with more targeted allocation. To address this mismatch, we formulate batch-level token allocation as constrained optimization problem and introduce CLEAR. CLEAR assigns zero budget to queries with negative expected surplus and reallocates tokens to queries where additional computation is more likely to improve utility. Extensive experiments show that CLEAR improves the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier, with the largest gains appearing in resource-scarce regimes where uniform allocation spreads computation too thinly. Future work will focus on narrowing the gap toward oracle-level allocation by improving the robustness of utility modelling. Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of LLM and machine learning. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

sections.title: Acknowledgements
sections.content: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 72571007 and Grant 72595830/72595831.

Appendix: Algorithm Details A.1. Pseudocode We present the pseudocode for the CLEAR in Algorithm ??, which computes the optimal resource allocation vector under given total budget constraint. A.2. Allocation Policy Details In this section, we formally define the token allocation ti for batch of queries = {s1, . . . , sN } under total budget constraint Btotal. Let ˆτi denote the predicted emergence threshold used by practical policies, and let di denote the ground-truth solution length used only by the Oracle policy. The average budget per query is = Btotal/N . A.2.1. UN R POLICY This is the standard baseline where resources are distributed equally, agnostic to task complexity. = B. tunif (19) A.2.2. PR C R-BASED PROPORTIONAL POLICY This policy assumes linear relationship between the latent threshold and required tokens, scaling the budget proportionally to the predicted length without abandonment. tprop = ˆτi (cid:80)N j=1 ˆτj Btotal. (20) In practice, we clip the allocation to minimum (e.g., 32 tokens) and maximum context length. A.2.3. TALE-EP POLICY TALE-EP first uses stronger teacher model to estimate perquery completion needs, then enforces both soft promptlevel budget constraint and hard decoding cap during regeneration. Let τi denote the raw budget estimate from the teacher model (e.g., Qwen3-30B-Instruct). 1. Teacher-based budget estimation: For each query si, the teacher predicts raw token need τi. 2. Renormalized proportional allocation: The raw estimates are converted into per-query caps under the same total budget: ˆttale = τi (cid:80)N j=1 τj Btotal. (21) In practice, we apply clipping and integerization: = clip(cid:0)(cid:4)ˆttale ttale (cid:7) , tmin, tmax (cid:1) , (22) followed by residual correction step to ensure exact budget conservation: (cid:88) i=1 ttale = Btotal. (23) 3. Budget-conditioned regeneration: For each query si, we construct budget-aware prompt with soft instruction (e.g., keep the entire response within at most ttale completion tokens), and run decoding with hard cap: yi pθ( si, ttale ), max tokens = ttale use We greedy (temperature = 0, top = 1). decoding in our . (24) setup Final accuracy is computed by applying math verify to regenerated outputs {yi}N i=1. A.2.4. CLEAR(HE S C) POLICY This baseline uses fixed cutoff rule based on the predicted thresholds. When the average budget is too small relative to the average predicted threshold, it drops the harder half of the batch and reallocates the budget to the remaining queries. 1. Check budget scarcity: Let ˆT = {ˆτ1, . . . , ˆτN } and define η = B/E[ ˆT ]. If η < 0.8, the cutoff rule is activated. 2. Select easier queries: Queries with ˆτi > Median( ˆT ) receive zero budget. The remaining queries share the total budget using: theur = (cid:40) µsur + κ (ˆτi µsur) 0 if ˆτi Median( ˆT ) otherwise, (25) where µsur is the mean predicted threshold among selected queries, and κ is chosen so that the selected queries use exactly Btotal tokens in total. 11 Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Figure 10. Predictor Performance Analysis on Qwen3-30BA3B-Instruct. C. Appendix: Data Composition and Statistics Training and Test Data Table 6 and Table 7 provide detailed breakdown of the datasets used for training the threshold predictor and evaluating the budget allocation policies. To ensure the robustness of our CLEAR framework, the evaluation suite is intentionally designed to be Out-ofDistribution (OOD) relative to the predictors training set. A.2.5. CLEAR(AU O N) POLICY This ablation uses simple greedy selection rule. It first keeps the queries with smaller predicted thresholds, since they are more likely to be completed under limited budget, and then distributes the available tokens among the selected queries. 1. Sort by predicted length: Sort the query indices by increasing predicted threshold, yielding permutation (p1, . . . , pN ) such that ˆτp1 ˆτpN . 2. Select survivors: Keep the largest prefix of this sorted list whose predicted thresholds fit within the total budget: = max [1, ] (cid:88) j= ˆτpj Btotal . (26) 3. Allocate to survivors: Assign zero budget to all nonselected queries. The selected queries then share the full budget using the same affine allocation rule as CLEAR (Heuristic), rescaled so that (cid:80) ti = Btotal. A.2.6. OR E POLICY This policy is an upper-bound baseline that uses groundtruth solution lengths di, which are unavailable at test time. It spends tokens on the shortest solvable queries first, maximizing the number of completed tasks under the total budget. 1. Sort by true length: Sort indices into permutation (o1, . . . , oN ) such that do1 doN . 2. Fill the budget greedily: Allocate exactly doj tokens to each query in sorted order until the next query would exceed Btotal: toracle oj = (cid:40) doj 0 l=1 dol Btotal if (cid:80)j otherwise. (27) B. Appendix: More Results To demonstrate the generalizability of our framework to larger-scale reasoning models, we present additional experimental results using Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct. Figure 10 validates the threshold predictors accuracy on this larger model, while Table 4 details the downstream allocation performance across varying supply-demand scenarios. In summary, CLEAR consistently outperforms the uniform baseline across all tested conditions, achieving up to +2.4 accuracy gains in strictly constrained budget environments where efficient resource distribution is most critical. 12 Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs Table 4. Performance Comparison across Difficulty Scenarios and Budgets on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct. We compare our proposed CLEAR against Uniform, Direct Predictor, and heuristic CLEAR variants. Results are reported as accuracy (%). Bold indicates the best performance among non-oracle methods. The bottom row shows the absolute accuracy gain of CLEAR over the Uniform baseline.
